title: Do LLMs Build Spatial World Models? Evidence from Grid-World Maze Tasks
venue: arXiv preprint (cs.AI), arXiv:2604.10690v1, submitted 12 Apr 2026; not otherwise published as of fetch date (2026-07-22)
claim: The paper argues that LLMs do not build robust, format-invariant spatial world models. Instead, spatial reasoning is representation-dependent (adjacency-list text vastly outperforms visual-grid text for the same maze) and prompting-dependent (base prompting fails for most models; explicit CoT/reasoning recovers performance), and models treat repeated or compositionally related spatial queries as independent events rather than reusing previously computed spatial facts, despite high semantic-coverage similarity in their reasoning traces (Abstract; Sec 5).
novelty: The authors present (1) a systematic evaluation framework manipulating maze representation (adjacency list vs. visual grid), model architecture, k-shot count (0/3/5), and prompting strategy (base/CoT/post-hoc reasoning) across 4 commercial LLMs and 5 grid sizes (5x5-9x9); (2) two new reasoning-consistency probes beyond path-finding -- Task 2 (sequential proximity questions with an exact-repeat query, Q3=Q0) and Task 3 (compositional distance comparison where Q2 could reuse Q0/Q1 results) -- explicitly designed to test information reuse/persistence rather than one-shot accuracy; (3) a dissociation finding that reasoning-trace 'semantic coverage' (96-99%) does not predict actual information reuse (much lower ROUGE-L, esp. DeepSeek-Chat as low as 0.08); (4) release of an open evaluation platform, SpatialBench, on HuggingFace Spaces with an interactive leaderboard (Sec 1 contributions list; Sec 5).

protocol.description: All three tasks are single forward-pass, full-observation prompts: the model is shown the complete maze (adjacency list or visual grid) plus the full task (path request, or a batch of 3-4 proximity questions) in one prompt and produces its entire answer (a coordinate path, or True/False answers to all questions in order) in one response. There is no multi-turn interaction loop, no incremental observation, and no environment feedback between steps -- reasoning (CoT / post-hoc) happens inside the single generation, not across separate API calls (Sec 3.1-3.3).
protocol.observation_content: Task 1 (Maze Navigation): the model sees the full maze in one of two formats in a single prompt -- (a) Adjacency List format with special tokens, e.g. '<ADJLIST_START> (0,0) <--> (0,1) (0,1) <--> (1,1) <ADJLIST_END> <ORIGIN_START> (0,0) <TARGET_START> (4,4) <PATH_START> ... <PATH_END>' (Sec 3.2), or (b) Visual Grid format, e.g. 'Row 0: [".", "S", ".", "#"] ... Legend: "#" = walls, "." = open paths, "S" = start, "E" = end' (Sec 3.2). For CoT, the model additionally receives the instruction: 'Before providing your final answer, think step-by-step about how to solve this maze: (1) First, analyze the maze structure and identify the start and end positions (2) Look for obvious paths and potential obstacles (3) Consider different possible routes (4) Choose the shortest valid path (5) Verify that your path does not cross walls or go out of bounds' (Sec 3.3). Output is the full coordinate path in one response: '(0,0) (0,1) (1,1) ...' (Sec 3.3). Task 2 (Sequential Reasoning with Point Reuse): full maze (adjacency list) plus grid-system legend plus four proximity questions Q1-Q4 all shown together in one prompt (Appendix A.3 example); model answers all four in one response, True/False plus rationale. Task 3 (Compositional Distance Comparison): full maze plus three questions about corner/center distances shown together in one prompt, with an explicit instruction to reuse intermediate reasoning across questions ('Solving one question may help with another, and you may reuse intermediate reasoning and calculations', Appendix A.4); model answers all three in one response. No partial/incremental observation, no per-step feedback (e.g., no distance-to-wall feedback loop), and no separate calls per question -- everything relevant is present in the single input the model conditions on.

representations.formats_compared: Two textual input formats for the same underlying maze: (1) Adjacency List (special-token delimited list of cell-to-cell edges plus origin/target markers, e.g. '<ADJLIST_START> (0,0) <--> (0,1) ... <ADJLIST_END> <ORIGIN_START> (0,0) <TARGET_START> (4,4)') and (2) Visual Grid (human-readable 2D character array with legend: '#'=wall, '.'=open, 'S'=start, 'E'=end) (Sec 3.2). Both formats remain text-based; no image/pixel input or coordinate-list-only format is tested (Sec 5 Discussion).
representations.effect_on_accuracy: Adjacency list substantially outperforms visual grid under CoT/reasoning prompting for 3 of 4 models. Headline: Gemini-2.5-Flash CoT reaches 86% on 5x5 adjacency-list mazes vs. 34% on visual-grid mazes (a 2-5x gap); on 7x7, Gemini reaches 80% adjacency vs. 16% visual (Sec 4.1; Table 4). GPT-5-mini is the exception and does not show this adjacency-list advantage (Sec 4.1). The paper interprets this as evidence of representation-dependent (not format-invariant) spatial reasoning.

models.name: Gemini-2.5-Flash
models.key_results: 5x5 CoT: 86% adjacency-list vs 34% visual (Sec 4.1; Table 4). 7x7 CoT: 80% adjacency vs 16% visual (Sec 4.1; Table 4). Base (0-shot) 5x5 adjacency only 8% (Table 4). Sequential Q3=Q0 repeat accuracy 0.98 (5x5) down to 0.74 (9x9) (Table 7). Reasoning-trace semantic coverage 0.91-0.96 but ROUGE-L only 0.57-0.85 across grid sizes (Table 1). Compositional task: Q2 shows +2.6 pts over avg(Q0,Q1), the largest positive delta among models (Table 9); overall compositional accuracy 79.1% (highest of the four) (Sec 4.3; Table 9).

models.name: GPT-5-mini
models.key_results: Does not show the adjacency-list advantage seen in other models -- 'Only GPT-5-mini's performance did not conform to this observation' (Sec 4.1). 5x5 CoT: 30% adjacency vs 32% visual (Table 4). Sequential Q3 repeat accuracy 1.00 (5x5) down to 0.42 (9x9), high ROUGE-L (0.52-0.89) indicating full re-computation each time rather than reuse (Table 1; Sec 4.2.1). Compositional task shows a decline at Q2 relative to avg(Q0,Q1): Delta = -2.2 (Table 9); per-grid-size Q0 accuracy drops sharply with grid size, from 100% at 5x5 to 4% at 9x9 (Table 8); the size-averaged aggregate Q0 value reported in Table 9 is 52.0% (overall compositional accuracy across all three questions is 51.5%, the lowest of the four models) (Table 8; Table 9).

models.name: Claude-Haiku-4.5
models.key_results: Base prompting: near-total failure, 0.00 accuracy on most 5x5/7x7/8x8/9x9 settings (Table 4). CoT recovers to 78% on 5x5 adjacency list, 74% on 6x6 adjacency ('Claude-Haiku-4.5's performance recovers to an accuracy of 78% with the chain-of-thought instruction', Sec 4.1; Table 4). Sequential task ROUGE-L notably low (0.36-0.40) despite SC 0.85-0.87, with explicit reuse language: model says 'From my previous analysis' and reuses computed distances in Q3 (Sec 4.2.1; Table 1). Compositional task: positive Q2 delta of +1.6 (Table 9); overall compositional accuracy 66.5% (Table 9).

models.name: DeepSeek-Chat
models.key_results: Base prompting largely fails (0.00-0.16 range across sizes) (Table 4); CoT recovers to 74% on 5x5 adjacency (Sec 4.1; Table 4). Sequential task: lowest ROUGE-L of all models, down to 0.08 at 9x9, despite high SC (~0.81-0.86) -- 'minimal reasoning in the repeated question, as the model saved the detailed process and only outputs the final answer' (Sec 4.2.1; Table 1). Compositional task: negative Q2 delta of -2.4 (Table 9); overall compositional accuracy 76.0% (Table 9).

headline_numbers: Gemini-2.5-Flash CoT: 86% (5x5 adjacency list) vs 34% (5x5 visual grid) -- 2-5x gap (Abstract; Sec 4.1; Table 4).
Gemini-2.5-Flash CoT on 7x7: 80% adjacency vs 16% visual (Sec 4.1; Table 4).
Claude-Haiku-4.5: near-0% accuracy with base prompting on 5x5 tokenized mazes, recovering to 78% with CoT (Abstract; Sec 4.1; Table 4).

All models show 96-99% semantic coverage in reasoning traces on the sequential point-reuse task, yet ROUGE-L drops as low as 0.08 (DeepSeek-Chat, 9x9), showing high conceptual similarity without literal information reuse (Abstract; Sec 4.2.1; Table 1).
Compositional distance comparison task: overall accuracy ranges 51.1%-79.1% across the four models, all above the 50% random-guess floor (Sec 4.3; Table 9).

Fine-tuned T5 baseline (Appendix, not an LLM prompting result): best in-distribution (matched train/test grid size) path accuracy is 0.806 (5x5-trained, 5x5-tested), with in-distribution accuracy declining as grid size grows (6x6: 0.769; 7x7: 0.691; 8x8: 0.614; 9x9: 0.537); the 0.856 figure in Table 3 is actually an out-of-distribution cell (6x6-trained model tested on 5x5, i.e. testing on a smaller grid than trained), not an in-distribution value; performance also collapses when testing on much larger grids than trained (5x5-trained model tested on 9x9: 0.093) (Appendix A.1; Table 3).
Performance consistently degrades as maze size grows from 5x5 to 9x9 across all tasks and models (Sec 4, Figure 2 description).
failure_modes: Without CoT/explicit reasoning instructions, models 'tend to end up getting lost in the maze after finding the shortest Euclidean distance, ignoring the maze grid structure and wall obstacles, as this is the most common failure mode' (Sec 4.1).
Representation brittleness: identical underlying maze structure produces a 2-5x accuracy swing purely from visual-grid vs adjacency-list formatting (Sec 4.1; Abstract).

Independent-question treatment / lack of state persistence: on the exact-repeat query (Q3=Q0), Gemini and GPT-5-mini fully re-execute the search from scratch each time (high ROUGE-L, re-derivation of BFS/path each call) rather than reusing prior computation (Sec 4.2.1).

Under-reasoning on repeats: DeepSeek-Chat and Claude-Haiku-4.5 show markedly lower ROUGE-L (down to 0.08 for DeepSeek) -- interpreted as reduced re-derivation, but this does not straightforwardly translate into higher accuracy or genuine cross-question memoization (Sec 4.2.1).

Compositional non-reuse: GPT-5-mini and DeepSeek-Chat show a performance decline at the composable question (Q2) relative to the average of its constituent sub-questions (Q0, Q1), rather than a benefit, with deltas of -2.2 and -2.4 respectively (Sec 4.3; Table 9).
k-shot learning often degrades performance on larger mazes or with longer context -- more few-shot examples do not reliably help and can hurt (Sec 5.1 Limitations).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper's entire experimental design is single-shot/single-forward-pass in the sense that each maze-solving or reasoning trial is one prompt-to-completion call; there is no interactive/multi-turn environment loop with incremental observations or corrective feedback anywhere in the study (Sec 3.1-3.3; confirmed no mention of any environment-in-the-loop or step-by-step API calls). Within that single-shot regime, the paper's central conclusion is about *within-prompt* multi-step reasoning: models can perform creditable one-shot spatial planning (up to 86% on 5x5 adjacency-list mazes) only when CoT or explicit reasoning is invoked in that same single call, but the same models fail to carry state across multiple *related* single-shot queries batched in one prompt (Task 2/3) -- i.e., they do not build a persistent internal spatial state that survives to a repeated or compositional question, even though all the information needed is present in the same context window. The paper explicitly frames this as models treating 'each question independently rather than building cumulative spatial knowledge' (Abstract) -- a failure of cross-query reuse within a single extended context, not a failure across genuinely separate interactive turns.

relation_to_candidate.quadrant: Full-observation x single-shot. Every task in this paper (maze path-finding, sequential point-reuse, compositional distance comparison) presents the complete maze plus the complete query in one prompt and elicits one full response -- no interactive/partial-observation loop is used anywhere in the paper.
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c19: Strong structural overlap: both use small (5x5-9x9 here vs. ~6x6 for c19) seeded ASCII/text grid-world mazes shown in full at once, both compare representation formats (this paper: adjacency-list vs. visual-grid text; c19: ASCII grid rendering), both use exact-match scoring against an independently computed oracle/ground truth, and both are motivated by testing whether models build genuine internal state/world models versus surface heuristics. This paper's Task 1 (predict/produce a full path) is the closest analog to c19's setup but asks for a path rather than a single derived fact; this paper's compositional/sequential tasks (predicting a True/False proximity relation) are closer in spirit to c19's single-derived-fact framing.
relation_to_candidate.differences: c19 asks for exactly ONE derived fact per instance (final coordinate/heading/carrying-flag) via a single token, deliberately removing path-validity/many-valid-answers ambiguity; this paper's Task 1 instead scores full path generation against exact ground-truth path match, which is a stricter and more path-ambiguous target. c19 introduces invented commands (jump-two, wrap) and a wrap-vs-clamp convention layer as adversarial strata to prevent naive-prompt flooring; this paper does not vary movement-command semantics -- its mazes use standard cell-adjacency movement only, with no invented commands or edge-wrap/clamp conditions. This paper additionally probes cross-query state persistence (Tasks 2-3, repeated/compositional questions in one prompt) and reasoning-quality dissociation (semantic coverage vs. ROUGE-L vs. LLM-judge) via three metrics; c19 has no analogous reasoning-trace-quality axis in the currently described design. This paper spans a formal 5x5-9x9 size sweep and three prompting strategies (base/CoT/post-hoc) plus k-shot (0/3/5); c19 as documented does not detail an equivalent CoT/k-shot ablation matrix.
relation_to_candidate.what_c19_would_add: A single-derived-fact, single-token exact-match target that eliminates path-validity ambiguity entirely (this paper's Task 1 still requires exact full-path match, which conflates planning correctness with state-tracking correctness); an adversarial invented-command and wrap-vs-clamp convention layer specifically engineered so no single fixed convention statement covers the whole stratified pool (this paper uses only standard, undisclosed-but-fixed adjacency movement rules, so a model that has memorized 'standard maze semantics' is not stress-tested against convention variation the way c19's design intends); and a much smaller, purpose-built oracle/harness (roughly 150 lines on top of Minigrid) suited to cheap, contamination-resistant quick-testing, versus this paper's heavier multi-metric (SC/ROUGE-L/LLM-judge) reasoning-quality apparatus, which is out of scope for a quick single-fact adopt-style task.

corpus_claim: This 2026 paper uses synthetic five-by-five to seven-by-seven grid mazes where accuracy swings from sixteen to eighty-six percent by representation and prompt format, validating c19's format-sensitivity and headroom claims. (Corpus title omits 'Evidence from'; models tested were Gemini-2.5-Flash, GPT-5-mini, Claude-Haiku-4.5, DeepSeek-Chat.)
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The 16%-86% swing and 5x5-7x7 grids are correctly cited as the paper's headline comparison (Gemini-2.5-Flash: 86% on 5x5 adjacency-list CoT vs 34% visual on 5x5, and 80% adjacency vs 16% visual on 7x7 -- Sec 4.1, Table 4), and the four model names are correct. However, '5x5 to 7x7' understates the paper's actual scope: the full experiments span grid sizes 5x5 through 9x9 (Sec 3.2, 'For each grid size n in {5,6,7,8,9}'; Table 4-9), with 5x5-7x7 being only the range highlighted in the abstract for the specific 86%/34%/80%/16% headline comparison, per the extraction header's own note. The title correction (paper's real title includes 'Evidence from') is correctly flagged as a minor discrepancy in the corpus claim's parenthetical, matching the extraction's title_discrepancy field. So the specific numbers and models are accurate, but the grid-size range as stated is a narrowed/incomplete characterization of the study's actual coverage.
baseline_relevant: Grid sizes used: 5x5, 6x6, 7x7, 8x8, 9x9 (n in {5,6,7,8,9}), 50 test mazes per size, generated via depth-first search with percolation p=0.2 for cycles/multiple paths (Sec 3.2).

Prompt formats that swung accuracy: Adjacency-list-with-special-tokens format vastly outperforms Visual-Grid-with-legend format under CoT for 3/4 models (86% vs 34% on 5x5 for Gemini) -- suggests our baseline should test both a structured/symbolic representation and a rendered-grid representation and expect large swings (Sec 3.2, 4.1; Table 4).

Prompting strategy floor/ceiling: base (no-CoT) prompting produces near-total failure for Claude-Haiku-4.5 and DeepSeek-Chat (0.00 on many settings) while CoT recovers them to 74-78% on 5x5 -- i.e., naive prompting alone is not a reliable floor-setter; explicit reasoning instructions are necessary to get any signal at all for some models (Sec 4.1; Table 4).

Metric used: exact-match accuracy (proportion of generated paths exactly matching ground-truth solution, or proportion of correct True/False answers) -- directly analogous to c19's exact-match scoring against an oracle (Sec 3.4.1).

Floors/ceilings observed: performance monotonically degrades with grid size across all tasks/models (Sec 4, Figure 2 description); compositional-reasoning task floor sits above chance (51.1%-79.1%, vs 50% random guess) even though models show no consistent evidence of true composition (Sec 4.3; Table 9).
k-shot examples can hurt, not help, especially on larger mazes/longer context (Sec 5.1) -- relevant if our baseline considers few-shot exemplars as a difficulty lever.

Contamination/data-release status: mazes are synthetically generated per-run via the maze-dataset library (Ivanitskiy et al., 2025) with DFS + percolation, not drawn from a fixed released benchmark file, which the paper frames as enabling reproducibility with 'provably correct solutions' (Sec 3.2, Sec 5) -- consistent with c19's own contamination-proofing via randomized seeded grids. The paper does release its own platform, SpatialBench, on HuggingFace Spaces (Sec 5), which is a public artifact but is separate from any pre-existing contaminated benchmark.

Both input formats tested remain text-based (no pixel/image input tested) -- the paper explicitly flags this as an open limitation for transfer to real visual-perception settings (Sec 5 Discussion), relevant if we ever consider a visual-rendering stratum for c19.
quotes: "Using chain-of-thought prompting, Gemini achieves 80-86% accuracy on smaller mazes (5x5 to 7x7 grids) with tokenized adjacency representations, but performance collapses to 16-34% with visual grid formats, which is a 2-5x difference, suggesting representation-dependent rather than format-invariant spatial reasoning." (Abstract)

"For example, Gemini-2.5-Flash with CoT achieves 86% on 5x5 tokenized mazes but only 34% on visual grid format... For example, Claude-Haiku-4.5 fails almost completely on 5x5 tokenized mazes with base prompting, yet recovers to 78% with CoT, demonstrating that spatial reasoning capabilities require clear instructions." (Sec 1 Introduction)
"For each grid size n in {5,6,7,8,9}, we generated 50 test mazes with guaranteed start-to-goal connectivity." (Sec 3.2)

"Visualizations of the model-generated paths demonstrate that the model tends to end up getting lost in the maze after finding the shortest Euclidean distance, ignoring the maze grid structure and wall obstacles, as this is the most common failure mode." (Sec 4.1)

"When using CoT or reasoning prompts, 3/4 models achieve substantially higher accuracy on adjacency lists compared to visual character grids... This gap persists across grid sizes: on 7x7 mazes, Gemini achieves 80% with adjacency list representation, compared to 16% with the visual representation. ... Only GPT-5-mini's performance did not conform to this observation." (Sec 4.1)

"Despite achieving 96-99% semantic coverage in reasoning traces, models fail to leverage this understanding for consistent spatial computations, indicating that they treat each question independently rather than building cumulative spatial knowledge." (Abstract)

"Overall performance ranged from 51.1% to 79.1%, with all models performing well above a random guess (50%). However, the key measuring metric here is Q2's accuracy relative to the average accuracy of Q0 and Q1." (Sec 4.3)

"Additionally, both input formats in our experiments remain text-based. While this controlled design isolates representational effects from perceptual ones, it leaves open the question of how these findings transfer to settings involving actual visual perception or 3D spatial reasoning." (Sec 5 Discussion)

corrections: GPT-5-mini model entry: dossier claimed 'overall compositional accuracy drops sharply with grid size (52.0% Q0 at 5x5 down to 4% at 9x9) (Table 8)', which conflated two different tables. Table 8's actual per-grid-size Q0 row for GPT-5-mini is 1.00 (5x5), 0.96 (6x6), 0.44 (7x7), 0.16 (8x8), 0.04 (9x9) -- so 5x5 Q0 accuracy is 100%, not 52.0%. The value 52.0 is Table 9's size-averaged aggregate Q0 score (mean of the five Table 8 values, expressed as a percentage), not a 5x5-specific figure, and Table 9 (not Table 8) is its actual source. Fixed to correctly attribute each number to its source table and distinguish the per-size trend (100%->4%) from the size-averaged aggregate (52.0%).

headline_numbers T5 baseline claim: dossier claimed 'path accuracy up to 0.856 in-distribution (6x6-trained, 6x6-tested)' from Table 3. This is incorrect: Table 3's Train=6x6/Test=6x6 diagonal (true in-distribution) cell is 0.769, not 0.856. The 0.856 value is actually Train=6x6/Test=5x5 (an out-of-distribution cross-size cell, testing on a grid smaller than trained), mislabeled as in-distribution. Fixed to report the correct in-distribution diagonal values (0.806/0.769/0.691/0.614/0.537 for 5x5-9x9) and to correctly characterize 0.856 as an out-of-distribution cell.

Minor quote fidelity fix: the quotes array rendered the Sec 5 Discussion sentence as starting with 'Both input formats...', but the source sentence actually reads 'Additionally, both input formats in our experiments remain text-based...' (lowercase 'both', with a dropped lead-in clause 'Additionally,'). Corrected the quote to match the source verbatim, including the leading 'Additionally,'.
